items.q: Why are passwords stored as hashes rather than plaintext?
items.correct: Hashes are one-way - even an attacker who steals them cannot easily recover passwords.
items.misconception: Hashes are encryption that can be decrypted with the right key.
items.random_wrong: Hashes compress the data to save space.
items.why: Students confuse hashing with encryption. Hashes have NO reverse function - you can verify a password by re-hashing, but cannot decrypt the stored hash to get the original.

items.q: How many layers are in the OSI model?
items.correct: 7 (Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application).
items.why: Students confuse OSI (7 layers) with the TCP/IP model (4 layers). They are related but distinct - OSI is a theoretical reference model; TCP/IP is the practical Internet stack.

items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.q: Which describes a network in a single building?
items.why: It's LAN. “WAN” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many layers does the OSI model have?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 layers. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
